solution.p: Lets start by factoring r^{2} from each term
Note that this answer can be checked by using the FOIL (First Outside Inside Last) technique (exercise).
When factoring, we should always first look for any greatest common factors of the terms. Here we find that r^2 is such a factor, and we have

To factor the remaining trinomial further, note that its leading term and its final term are perfect squares. Their square roots are 10r and 1. After multiplying their square roots, we have 10r, which is half of the middle term. When all this happens, we have a perfect square trinomial, and the two square roots we found make the binomial that should be squared. That is,
